draft.introduction: Hymn 1 of Synesius of Cyrene (c. 400 CE) blends Neoplatonic philosophy with emerging Christian theology. Synesius, a student of the philosopher Hypatia who later became a bishop, represents the fluid boundary between pagan and Christian thought in late antiquity.

translation.text: Come, O lyre of mine, after your Teian songs, after your Lesbian, strike now a Dorian note, not leading the dance of youth or singing of wily Eros, but to hymn the wise begetter of the world, the self-born, self-originated king who sits upon the throne of truth beyond the vaults of heaven.

translator_notes.note: Synesius’s hymns are deliberately ambiguous: their language is compatible with both Neoplatonic and Christian theology. The “self-born king” could be the Neoplatonic One or the Christian God. This ambiguity was probably intentional—Synesius accepted the bishopric of Ptolemais on the condition that he not be required to abandon his philosophical convictions.
